speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, And we're in Sydney, Australia. [INAUDIBLE]. Camera is ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was then we, we were told that the Germans on their retreat are still at war. And they will shoot us all down. So that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this old man? And how were you [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: A soldier, a German soldier on the run, asked us to help him, to give him civilian clothes. But all they wanted-- and they did hang Winkler and his wife-- hung them-- caught them and hung them. And they said, Heil Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Winkler?

speaker: interviewee
text: The master of the [? weaving ?] plant in Merdzorf. And that is when I went into a house with my sister. No-- before that, we were all assembling as we were so drilled to stand in fives to be counted. So we again went out, because we didn't know. And the, the girls from Theresienstadt were old hands. They knew about concentration camps. And they were nice. They said, we take over now. The Polish ones have run away with the SS. And it will be now run by some Czechs and some Viennese people. Let's go and have a look what food we have. They found hundreds of open tins, our tins which the Polish kapos ate for themselves, you see, SS together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they found half a pound or maybe less, I don't understand, of potatoes. You can imagine the quality of potatoes in May when they were dug out in September-- black, sleazy, miserable, stinking thing. And she said, shall I boil it all, and everybody gets his share? Or will we have only two at a time? No, boil it all.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, if I have to die, I thought to myself, I die with a full stomach. My sister was in a sick bay already. She couldn't move anymore. She was a-- you heard her bones rattle. She was like a [? bells ?] horror. Her teeth were loose. I didn't think she'd live the end of the war. But we all waited for the Germans to come. And suddenly, the gates of the camp opened. And three men on little horses came in. And I thought, this is it. They're coming to shoot us. My sister will die anyhow. I go first. I don't want to see my friends in their blood. I go. And I stepped forward. They looked at me. And I saw on their ca a hammer and sickle. And I said, [RUSSIAN SPEECH]. And I started with my little Russian, which I taught myself, a little conversation. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean, what you just said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome. How is Comrade Stalin? And he said, he's all right. And suddenly, I didn't understand why they started to cry. We didn't-- you don't see each other. There were no mirrors in Merzdorf. And we were so used to each other. But for somebody-- this was the first camp they liberated. They couldn't understand. They couldn't understand what happened. And then they said, [RUSSIAN SPEECH]. Gitler-- they can't say H-- Gitler. And they showed the pouch for the revolver where they had a blooming lilac branch and said, [RUSSIAN SPEECH]. Go home. And then, as they said that, they were crying. They had bad marks in their faces. So I thought to myself. I started to think-- when he said [? domoi ?] that was the only time where I had a bit of an uneasy feeling in my stomach, which has repeated itself over the years with great vehemence, I assure you. But that uneasy feeling was when they said [? domoi. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: What home am I going to find? Where am I going to go? And I picked up my sister, [INAUDIBLE], and the girl, Johanna Orenstein, and Mrs. Irriza and her daughter, Stella. We were five. And we went into-- out of the camp. I was also only very young. But I thought if the whole Russian army marches into these village and they didn't see women for such a long time and these women didn't see men for such a long time, there is going to be something dreadful happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't quite know what I was frightened of. But I was a bit uneasy. I wasn't scared. I walked into the first house, and there was a woman from the lamp hanging down. So whether they hung her or whether she hung herself, I do not know. I passed her. And I went into the living room, beautiful books everywhere. My sister curled on the couch somewhere. And I looked through the books, and I found [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I picked up the book. I lied on the hard floor, because I was so used to the tough boards that it took me months before I could sleep in a bed. And I started to read Scarlett O'Hara. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That's how it starts. And to me, the war was finished. I knew I live. I knew I can read. I knew there are books around. I knew I will go back to school. There must be laws. On what I believe, I did not know. But I knew I have survived, only with this reading the book. To me, that was the key for my survival, that I could read books again. And then I left my sister there-- when I think back of this-- and I marched and found the Russian camp. And I spoke a little bit of Russian. I taught myself. My father told me, you must learn the Russian. Forget the English, because we will come under the Russian interest zone here in Czechoslovakia as the war is going. And I said, I want a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a Jewish commander, a commander. They found one, a major, spoke Yiddish, to my bad luck, from Kiev.

speaker: interviewee
text: His whole family was murdered. He said to me, I do everything for you if you give me, as a souvenir, your number. Can I take this as a souvenir from you? I said, have it, by all means. And he-- I'm very hungry. And I have a sister and three other girls. We are five together. We are-- we want to go to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will help you. I bring you food first. And he came back in 10 minutes and brought a huge, big cake, a huge cake of gouda cheese, which I not only couldn't hold-- because it was too heavy for me-- not a knife to cut it, no-- neither strength to do it. And that is the story of my life, that the first food which was offered to me, I couldn't accept because I couldn't eat it. And I was very wise that I didn't. Because the girls in the camps who stayed there-- and I believe it was very dirty there after-- they, to, as a victory celebration, they roasted an ox, which they requisitioned, on a spear. And they were sitting there waiting, the stupid women who didn't eat for years, and ate this half-cooked flesh and had busted stomach walls. And more died after the eighth of May than before. And then it all went very quick. One could catch a train with great difficulties. And I came to a first class compartment, because there was no room anywhere else. And there was a man sitting there by the window. And he was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] out of a white, beautiful serviette, having black bread and bacon. We walked in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still don't know how I collected all this chutzpah. All five, we sat down there. The man looked at us. He was disgusted. And I said to him, please, we haven't eaten for nine months. Can you give us the bread? Keep the bacon, if you want. But give us the bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said-- we just come out of a concentration camp. And he said to me in Czech, I didn't send you there. And he picked up the food and put it away in his briefcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comes the conductor-- tickets, please. I said, sorry, we are from the concentration camp. He had white gloves. He said, you can't sit in my first class. I said, who said so? He said, these are regulations. I said, this is war. And we are war orphans, all of us. And I'm very sorry. I'm not moving from here. But if you are so hungry for money, go to the International Red Cross and collect.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he left. And he let us sit there. And in style, very quickly we went through Brno, through Moravia. It took me about two days. And I went to-- back to Puchov. And, of course, Jankol Hantag, our Aryan friend, lived in our house, lived in our bed. I went off. I left my sister there to finish her schooling. And they, they kept her there because they owed us a lot of money, which they paid eventually. And it was all changed. It was old money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get your house and the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Half the business-- half was his. And the other half, he bought. And the money-- the currency lost value. And we had nothing. We had a war orphan pension after our father. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone else left from your family, Kitty?

speaker: interviewee
text: An uncle, my mother's cousin, Josef Haas, who was in Dachau. And he bitterly cried when he saw us without hair. He didn't look much better. His wife died in his arms. He found her in Ramsberg. And he died, only a few years ago, when he was 80. And I correspond-- he had Roman Catholic family, a son and a daughter, all very Catholic. He married a Catholic girl in second marriage, who died of leukemia. He-- and he brought up the children by himself. And that is my only connection with Slovakia, that twice a year, I write her a rude letter about the Slovaks. And she tells me how she saw and how she cried. And I have nothing to say to her. And my sister and I decided that this is no future for us. And there was a possibility to come to Australia as a war orphan scheme. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say you left your sister in Puchov and went elsewhere yourself. Where did you go, and what were you looking--

speaker: interviewee
text: To [INAUDIBLE], to a next town, and I did my, my living with distinction. They gave me [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the toughest translation you can have. They made it so hard for me. And then the examining professor-- we do it all orally in Czechoslovakia-- and then, then for examining, professor said, how many Jews do you think have been deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, six million from what I read in the papers. He said, well, when I hear you translating here, I think seven million came out the other end of the chimney. And I-- we were so unwelcome, so unwelcome, so hated. People would meet me in Puchov in the street. And I say, good day. And they say, oh, you have come back? We heard that they murdered you all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we decided we will emigrate totally. I spoke good English by that time. I studied it at the University in Prague. I had a scholarship. My sister studied kindergarten teacher, had a scholarship. We had a room. We share the room. We managed. The Joint Distribution Committee helped us to get our landing permits and our tickets. And and, we came-- on the 28th of May, '49. I left Czechoslovakia totally unprepared for immigration. I didn't have a trade. I didn't have a profession. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you graduated from University in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was expelled, as per usual, because I was not in the party. So we were-- 40,000 were dis-- expelled. And I could now go back. There is a big amnesty. And all these people could go back and graduate and do their doctorates. But anti-Semitism now is worse, I think, than before the war. Because people are claiming restitution. So did I. So did everybody else. And that caused enormous anti-Semitism, because they don't want to back-- give back the plunder. And then we went to Genoa in Italy and waited there. I met people who had money, people who did black business, people who had gold, people who had paid for their fare, people who put their act together after the war. Couples-- there were no single girls, only very few, a few boys. Everybody tried to marry before you went. Everybody was too frightened to go alone, just like this, to Australia. My sister was very popular. I had a boyfriend in Prague, Otto Ruzdichka. He's in Papua, New Guinea. I helped him to immigrate many, many years ago. He's a very wealthy man. And so in every port, I had letters from him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was really looking forward. We got $10 from the American Joint in Genoa, $10 American dollars in single dollar bills. And I said to my sister, do you think that will buy us a house in Australia? And she's-- and when the boat, the SS Continental, on the 1st of July, 1949, drove-- sailed under the Harbour Bridge that you think that they will hit it, I looked at the little houses on the shore. And I looked at the higher houses on the shore. And I said to my sister, I don't think we will make a house. But maybe a flat we could rent here with our money. So the Jewish Welfare rent a room for us and paid for a fortnight. We arrived on a Thursday. And on Monday, I started work. They sent me to 250 Peat Street from 28 Edison Street, Kensington, which was like a trip to the North Pole. And I got there. And there was this Mr. Pfieffer who said to me, do you speak German? I said, yes. He said, that's good. This is a German Jewish firm. And can you count till 12? I said, I, I, I can try. I thought he had a sense of humor. He said, can you try till 144? I said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you will learn. Because this is a button shop. And 12 is a dozen. And 144 is a gross. And you are expected, for seven hours a day, six days a week. Five guineas, I pay you. I can't pay more. And you will be only counting buttons. And I did for three years. And then I got married to Kurt Fischer, who was driving taxis who was a very brilliant painter and interior decorator from Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Shanghai, he survived. And I met him here. It was the love of my life. And in 1954, we married. In 1956, my son was born. And in 1958, my husband left me. The baby was two years old. And I had a little children's wear shop in Kogarah. And that was burgled on Cup Day, 1965. And I couldn't carry on anymore. I got a very severe nervous breakdown. Because that was then that I started to feel a failure. I never had that feeling before. I just thought it was part of emigration that you do many menial jobs and that you have no social connections. I didn't need them. I got matriculation in one year at the Sydney University and went to the bloody Jewish Welfare Society, who helped me to come out and who were very kind to me. And Mr. Walter Brand-- Walter Brand was the president then. And I said, Mr. Brand, please let me study. Help me with a scholarship. And I pay it all back. I want to be a famous divorce lawyer. And I have one year recognized. Professor Anderson gave me credit of one year. That was a big thing. And he said, you speak English, Kitty. You're going to work. We have to look after people who don't speak the language. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I had recovered from the break down. Then, a lady offered to me-- I didn't, couldn't go back to the flat. It was too troublesome. Paulie was in boarding school because he couldn't be trusted to me anymore, according to my husband. And in a deep depression, I manned the night switch in a boarding house. And for that, I got free board and a room. And I was very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the man said to me one day, would you like to give me a wake up call 6:00 in the morning? I said, be merciful. Where are you going? He said, to New Guinea. I said, where is that? He said, north of Australia. And I have great [? pastoral ?] interest there. And I'll be back in a fortnight. I said, see where you can find me a job. He said, what could you do? I said, I could teach. I speak languages. And I would like to go there. I need to go back from Sydney. And I need to start a new life.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'll let you know. He came back a fortnight later. And he told me that he spoke to somebody at the Department of External Territories. He told me exactly what to do. You must have a return ticket. But they'll refund you if you get the job. You must go by boat and get a permit, which he got me. And so mad as a meat ax, in the middle of a tragic breakdown, I kissed my son goodbye. I boarded the [? Brayside ?] on Yom Kippur, 1966. I came alone on the boat. And two old ladies visited me later on the boat. I rung my son. He said, Mummy, don't forget to write.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the captain came and said to me, sprechen sie Deutsch? Do you speak German? I said, yes. He said, come. And we went up and up and up and up until we came into the radio officer's room. And he said, Ziggy, there is somebody for you. She is going to Port Moresby. And they got up in snow white startched uniform, a young man. And he said, Otto Hermann Siegfried Rozeroff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was such a fright, me and my breakdown. And I packed 200 Valium tablets, because I had it all worked out. I had no intention of going to New Guinea. I was going to commit suicide on the boat. And I will swallow all these tablets. And I will be buried at sea so that I'm of no embarrassment to my son. That's all what worries me, that he should never see me in that terrible [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had it all planned. And I said it all to the Ziggy straight away. And he told-- was as old as me then. And he told me that he-- all he wanted to be is a captain in the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] merchant navy. And then the end of the war and no captain, no German navy. And he escaped to the Foreign Legion. And he said, what's your cabin number? I said, number 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I come and see you. And we will talk more about your Valium tablets. And that Otto Hermann Siegfried Rozeroff sat with me every night after duty. And, one day, we took the tablets. And we buried them at sea. And he is still this day my good friend. He's a complete alcoholic and in [INAUDIBLE] somewhere where I visit him sometimes, a highly intelligent, brilliant man. And every time, when the [? Brayside ?] came to New Guinea, I got a cable. Ziggy's coming. And they kept me alive in the beginning in Port Moresby, which was dreadful. I was in a girls' hostel where I didn't belong. I was the only Jewish person anybody ever saw. The bosses gave me a job as a business advisory officer, because I said I had a shop. They were sorry for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had more luck with people in life who were sorry for me than these nice people who respected me, which was very sorrowful to me. Because I would have rather been, been respected than feeling sorry for. And, in Port Moresby, I trained in bookkeeping. And to cut the long story short, they send me up to the highlands in Goroka and then had a bet amongst themselves. How long will Fischer last-- a month, two months?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't think-- they all knew that I was sick. Only I didn't know that I was sick. I was under no medication, under no doctor. And I had a manic depressive condition, which I didn't know I had. And they let me loose on humanity. And I volunteered to go into a leprosy hospital to put the leprosy station on a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] business-like level. And the we ordered spinning wheels from the National Council of Jewish Women in New Zealand, where they are very cheap. And I got it as a donation, 10 spinning wheels, the [INAUDIBLE] of the first loom in the Lutheran Technical College. Leon [? Fink, ?] he-- who died not so long ago-- sent me carpet residue for ponchos and for doormats. And I cannot sew a button on. I cannot do-- I cannot cook an egg. But I'm a brilliant organizer. That's what I learned there. And with this leprosy, if they had no fingers, they could weave with their legs. If they had fingers, they could spin. And every-- behind every of my project was always a nun and a bishop. It was my way to commemorate the happy days I had in my convent hiding time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean a nun and a bishop?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Catholic Church, that was not the Australian administration-- and the Catholic Church had the money. The Catholic Church had the know-how. And the Catholic Church was very powerful in Papua, New Guinea, very German, very Deutsche. They're singing German-- sing songs at school. And these leprosy sisters send me a German video to our Jewish benefactor. Today, 25 years later, they still remember me. They are very wealthy. They make beautiful table mats. And there, I learned to do things for others. I learned there community development. I learned then-- the government send me to Israel on a scholarship, the, the six them-- Yom Kippur War broke out. As soon as I got there, the war was on. And President Katzir, because I helped the cause together very [? well-- ?] there were all blacks from Africa, from Sierra Leone, from the White Ivory Coast, from everywhere. And I was representing Papua, New Guinea, a Kitty Fisher, a Jewish mama. When I came off the plane in Israel, the man who was sent to pick me up was from Tunis, to make it more difficult, spoke only French, no English. He said [FRENCH SPEECH]. I said, Kitty Fisher. I said, I am Kitty Fisher. No, no , no. [FRENCH SPEECH]. So there I was amongst all black savages, the only white Jewish woman during this dangerous time of the Yom Kippur War. And I listened to-- every night I got WIZO to come when they had a short-- when the news are on at 8:30, a terrible hour. They came from the WIZO, two ladies. And they translated us the news. Because we didn't know the news. Herzog, who is now president, was then chief commander of the army, who said always a few talk in English, English words. And so I prepared myself professionally for a diploma in community development. And that got me the job in Darwin in 1974 after the cyclone.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. OK, that's the end of tape four of the interview with Katarina Fischer. It's the 21st of March, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
